talk_title: High-plex 3D microscopy for spatial profiling of tumor microenvironments

models.name: light sheet fluorescence microscopy volumes
models.genotype_or_key_features: up to 2 mm thick human FFPE blocks

MOA_summary: The presenter described cyclic immunofluorescence–based high‑plex 3D microscopy (up to 150 channels) applied to thick (35 µm) FFPE sections and light‑sheet fluorescence imaging, enabling whole‑cell morphology, subcellular organelle localization, and spatial context (e.g., cytotoxic synapse distance, tertiary lymphoid structure architecture) to be captured in three dimensions, which resolves misidentification and provides functional readouts that are impossible in standard 2D sections.
key_findings: Thick (35‑40 µm) FFPE sections retain ~70 % of cells intact versus <5 % in standard 5 µm sections, dramatically reducing lineage misidentification.
3D imaging revealed that ~40 % of CD8+ T cells in tumors exhibit LAG‑3 staining only in deeper z‑planes, explaining false‑negative checkpoint marker detection in 2D slides.
Morphological analysis showed average T cells make 5–10 functional contacts with neighbors; synapse distances contract from ~1 µm to ~30 nm during cytolytic engagement.

Serial reconstruction of ~470 tertiary lymphoid structures in lung adenocarcinoma and tuberculosis demonstrated heterogeneous developmental stages within a single follicle, which 2D sections misclassify.
Light‑sheet 3D imaging of a 2 mm‑thick colon specimen captured ~30 million cells, allowing tracing of nerve‑immune interactions and vascular‑associated immune niches.

talk_title: AI approaches to quantitative analysis of 3D tissue imaging
MOA_summary: The talk outlined how foundation models, segmentation networks, synthetic‑staining GANs and diffusion models, and optical‑flow based slice interpolation are applied to 2D and 3D histopathology data (H&E, cyclic IF, light‑sheet) to generate cell‑level segmentations, virtual multiplex stains, and fill missing z‑planes, thereby enabling quantitative morphometric and spatial analyses that were previously infeasible.
readouts: synthetic multiplex IHC generated by diffusion models
key_findings: Foundation models can embed H&E tiles into high‑dimensional vectors that predict patient outcomes and highlight prognostic regions such as venous invasion without additional training.
A GAN‑based pipeline converts quantitative phase images into realistic H&E, allowing existing H&E‑trained models to be applied to label‑free imaging.
Diffusion models successfully synthesize multiplex IHC (e.g., insulin/glucagon) from a single H&E slide, enabling retrospective multi‑omic analysis of archived specimens.
Optical‑flow algorithms reconstruct missing intermediate sections in CODA and Visium datasets, improving continuity for 3D reconstructions and spatial transcriptomics.
AI‑driven nucleus and tissue segmentation using models like Segment‑Anything achieve >90 % concordance with manual annotations across multiple organ systems.
